name: Microsoft Entra ID User and Group Management
description: Creating, managing, and synchronizing users and groups in Microsoft Entra ID for Azure access control.
key_facts: Users can be cloud-only or synchronized from on-premises Active Directory via Entra Connect
Groups can be Security groups (for access) or Microsoft 365 groups (for collaboration)
Dynamic membership rules auto-assign users to groups based on user attributes
Bulk operations (bulk create, invite, delete) can be performed using CSV files in the portal
Guest accounts (B2B) allow external users to access your tenant's resources
common_misconceptions: Deleting a user in Azure does not immediately remove all their access; group memberships may persist
Dynamic groups automatically update membership but may have a delay of up to 24 hours

name: Azure Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)
description: Fine-grained access management for Azure resources using role assignments at various management scopes.
key_facts: RBAC role assignments have three parts: security principal, role definition, and scope
Scope hierarchy: Management Group > Subscription > Resource Group > Resource
Built-in roles: Owner, Contributor, Reader, User Access Administrator
Role assignments are additive; the most permissive role wins if multiple roles are assigned
Deny assignments override role assignments and block specific actions even for owners
common_misconceptions: Removing a role assignment takes effect immediately but may take up to 30 minutes to propagate to all tokens
Owner role does not grant all rights; certain actions like changing resource lock require Owner plus management lock permissions

name: Azure Policy and Management Groups
description: Governance controls for enforcing organizational standards and assessing compliance across Azure resources.
key_facts: Azure Policy evaluates resources and flags or blocks non-compliant configurations
Policy effects: Deny, Audit, Append, Modify, DeployIfNotExists, AuditIfNotExists
Initiatives (policy sets) group related policies for a compliance goal like CIS Benchmark
Management Groups organize subscriptions and apply policies/RBAC hierarchically
Policy remediation can automatically fix non-compliant existing resources via DeployIfNotExists
common_misconceptions: Audit effect logs non-compliance but does not block deployment; Deny effect prevents deployment
Policies do not override RBAC; they operate at the resource configuration level, not permission level

name: Azure Storage Accounts and Access
description: Managing Azure Storage accounts including replication, access tiers, security, and lifecycle management.
key_facts: Storage account types: Standard GPv2 (general), Premium (SSD-backed, low latency), BlobStorage (blob only)
Redundancy options: LRS (3 copies, 1 datacenter), ZRS (3 zones), GRS (2 regions), GZRS (zones + region)
Access is controlled via Storage Account Keys (full access), SAS tokens (delegated), or Azure RBAC
Lifecycle management policies automatically transition blobs between tiers or delete them based on age
Blob versioning preserves previous blob versions for recovery from accidental modifications
common_misconceptions: Storage Account Key access bypasses RBAC; key rotation should be automated to reduce exposure
GRS replicates to a paired region but the secondary is read-only unless a failover is initiated

name: Azure File Shares and Azure File Sync
description: Fully managed cloud file shares accessible via SMB/NFS and hybrid sync with on-premises Windows file servers.
key_facts: Azure Files provides SMB 2.1/3.0 and NFS 4.1 protocol support for cloud file shares
Azure File Sync caches Azure file shares on on-premises Windows servers to reduce cloud latency
Cloud tiering in File Sync keeps frequently accessed files locally and tiers cold files to the cloud
Share snapshots provide point-in-time read-only copies of Azure file share data
Azure Files supports identity-based authentication via Active Directory or Entra ID
common_misconceptions: Azure File Sync server endpoints can have cloud tiering disabled; all files will be cached locally
NFS Azure file shares require premium storage accounts and are not supported on Windows

name: Azure Virtual Machines Deployment and Management
description: Deploying, configuring, managing, and scaling Azure Virtual Machines for various workload types.
key_facts: VM sizes are organized into families: General Purpose (D), Compute Optimized (F), Memory Optimized (E/M), Storage Optimized (L)
Availability Sets distribute VMs across fault domains and update domains within a datacenter
VM Scale Sets automatically scale VM count based on demand rules or schedules
Managed disks handle storage replication automatically; OS disks use Premium or Standard SSD
Azure Spot VMs use spare capacity at discount but can be evicted with 30 seconds notice
common_misconceptions: Stopping (deallocating) a VM stops compute billing but still incurs storage and IP address costs
Availability Sets provide redundancy within a single datacenter; Availability Zones span separate datacenters

name: Azure App Service and Deployment Slots
description: PaaS platform for hosting web applications, REST APIs, and mobile backends with zero-downtime deployment features.
key_facts: App Service Plans define the compute resources (CPU, memory, instances) for hosting apps
Deployment slots are separate staging environments that can be swapped with production atomically
Auto-scaling in App Service uses rules based on CPU, memory, HTTP queue length, or schedule
Custom domains and SSL/TLS certificates can be bound to App Service apps
App Service supports multiple runtimes: .NET, Node.js, Python, Java, PHP, Ruby
common_misconceptions: Multiple apps can share the same App Service Plan and share its compute resources
Slot swap routes traffic to the new slot without downtime, but session state may be lost during the swap

name: Azure Virtual Network Design and Management
description: Planning, configuring, and managing Azure Virtual Networks including subnets, routing, and DNS.
key_facts: VNets are scoped to a region; address spaces use CIDR notation and cannot overlap when peering
Subnets divide the VNet address space and must be non-overlapping within the VNet
Azure reserves 5 IP addresses in each subnet: network, broadcast, and 3 Azure infrastructure addresses
User-defined routes (UDRs) override Azure default routing to control traffic paths
Service endpoints provide optimized routing to Azure PaaS services over the Azure backbone
common_misconceptions: VNet peering is not transitive; you must explicitly peer every pair of VNets that need to communicate
Private endpoints and service endpoints both restrict PaaS access, but private endpoints assign a private IP

name: Network Security Groups and Application Security Groups
description: Layer 4 network access control lists and application-centric groupings for simplifying security rule management.
key_facts: NSG rules filter traffic by priority (100-4096), lower number = higher priority, first match wins
NSGs can be associated with subnets or individual NICs; both can apply to a single resource
Default NSG rules allow VNet traffic and Azure Load Balancer; deny all other inbound by default
Application Security Groups (ASGs) group VMs logically so NSG rules reference ASGs instead of IPs
Effective Security Rules show the combined NSG rules from subnet and NIC associations
common_misconceptions: NSG rules with the same priority number across inbound and outbound are separate; they do not conflict
NSGs are stateful; allowing inbound port 80 automatically allows the return outbound traffic

name: Azure Load Balancing Services
description: Services that distribute network traffic across multiple backend resources for high availability and performance.
key_facts: Azure Load Balancer (Layer 4) distributes TCP/UDP traffic; Standard SKU supports zones and HTTPS health probes
Azure Application Gateway (Layer 7) load balances HTTP/HTTPS with SSL termination and WAF
Azure Traffic Manager uses DNS-based routing policies to distribute traffic across global endpoints
Azure Front Door provides global load balancing, WAF, and CDN capabilities in a single service
Health probes monitor backend health; unhealthy backends are removed from the pool automatically
common_misconceptions: Azure Load Balancer is internal or public, not both simultaneously; use separate instances for each
Traffic Manager is DNS-based and does not proxy traffic; latency includes DNS TTL caching delays

name: Azure Monitor and Log Analytics
description: Collecting, analyzing, and acting on telemetry data from Azure resources to maintain operational health.
key_facts: Azure Monitor collects metrics (numerical time-series) and logs (structured records) from resources
Diagnostic settings must be configured to route resource logs to Log Analytics, Storage, or Event Hubs
KQL (Kusto Query Language) is used to query Log Analytics workspaces
Alerts fire when metric thresholds are breached or log query results match conditions
Action Groups define who is notified and how (email, SMS, Azure Function, webhook) when an alert fires
common_misconceptions: Platform metrics are free; custom metrics and log data ingestion incur charges based on volume
Alerts are stateful; they fire once when a condition is met, not repeatedly while the condition persists

name: ARM Templates and Bicep for Infrastructure as Code
description: Declarative Infrastructure-as-Code tools for deploying and managing Azure resources consistently at scale.
key_facts: ARM templates are JSON files that declaratively define Azure infrastructure; idempotent deployments
Bicep is a domain-specific language that compiles to ARM JSON; shorter and more readable syntax
Template parameters allow reuse across environments; template outputs return values after deployment
ARM deployment modes: Incremental (default, adds/updates resources) and Complete (deletes unspecified resources)
Linked templates reference external template URLs for modular deployments
common_misconceptions: ARM templates are not procedural; they declare end state, and ARM determines the deployment order
Complete deployment mode deletes ALL resources in the resource group not in the template—use with care

name: Azure Backup and Site Recovery
description: Services for protecting data and applications with backup, restore, and disaster recovery capabilities.
key_facts: Azure Backup provides backup for VMs, SQL databases, Azure Files, and on-premises servers
Recovery Services Vault stores backup data with configurable retention policies
Azure Site Recovery replicates VMs to a secondary region for disaster recovery failover
Backup policies define backup frequency (daily, weekly) and retention (daily, weekly, monthly, yearly)
Instant restore allows restoring VM disks from snapshots within 1-5 days without waiting for vault retrieval
common_misconceptions: Azure Backup and Azure Site Recovery serve different purposes; Backup restores data, ASR fails over workloads
Backup policy changes apply to future backups only; existing recovery points retain their original retention

name: Azure Container Instances and AKS
description: Running containerized workloads in Azure using serverless containers or managed Kubernetes clusters.
key_facts: Azure Container Instances (ACI) runs containers without managing VMs; pay-per-second billing
Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS) manages Kubernetes control plane; customers manage worker nodes
Container images are stored in Azure Container Registry (ACR) and pulled during deployment
AKS node pools group VMs by SKU; system pools run critical pods, user pools run application pods
AKS integrates with Entra ID for RBAC and Azure Monitor for container insights
common_misconceptions: ACI and AKS serve different use cases; ACI is for simple single containers, AKS for complex orchestration
AKS control plane (API server, etcd) is managed by Microsoft; worker nodes are customer-managed VMs

name: Azure VPN Gateway and ExpressRoute
description: Connectivity options for linking on-premises networks and Azure VNets securely.
key_facts: Azure VPN Gateway creates encrypted IPsec/IKE tunnels between Azure VNets and on-premises networks
Site-to-Site VPN connects an on-premises network to Azure; Point-to-Site connects individual devices
ExpressRoute provides dedicated private connectivity from on-premises to Azure via a connectivity provider
ExpressRoute does not traverse the public internet, offering higher reliability, speed, and security
VPN Gateway SKUs determine maximum throughput and number of connections supported
common_misconceptions: VPN Gateway and ExpressRoute can coexist as active-active for resiliency or ExpressRoute failover
ExpressRoute bandwidth is shared in a circuit; multiple virtual connections (peerings) share the total bandwidth
